Researcher track — molecular evolution — primate sexual dichromatism

The sex-hormone signal in dichromatic primates concentrates in steroid-metabolism enzymes, not receptors

Across four independently evolved dichromatic clades, intensified selection (HyPhy RELAX, BH-corrected, QC-gated) lands on five interconverting androgen/estrogen-synthesis enzymes — HSD17B1, HSD17B12, HSD17B7, CYP7B1, SRD5A1 — while the androgen and estrogen receptors (AR, ESR1, ESR2) show no significant signal.

This pattern is biologically coherent for a trait that is, by definition, sex-specific: selection tuning how much active hormone is made, in a sexually dimorphic character, rather than how tissue responds to a fixed hormone level.

117 genomically-sampled primates · HyPhy RELAX intensification/relaxation test on the dichromatic foreground · per-gene Benjamini–Hochberg correction · QC gate (alignment coverage, K bounds, foreground n, tree length)

Design — why the unit of analysis is the origin, not the species

238 primates scored for dichromatism; 24 enter the genomic test; the 24 tips are not 24 independent events

Dichromatism was scored across a 238-species table (9 complete, 15 partial dichromatic; 93 monochromatic scored as background/reference). Pooling every dichromatic tip into one foreground presupposes a shared architecture across the trait — the very question under test. Ancestral-state reconstruction on the full tree resolves the 24 genomically-usable tips into 14 clades of origin (of up to ~14–15 phenotypic origins estimated across the complete 238-tip tree); the selection test itself is powered on the 4 clades with usable genomic sampling (Lemuridae/Eulemur, Cebidae, Hylobatidae, Cercopithecidae).

Limitation. Origin counts differ by tree scope: 4 clades carry a genome-level selection test; ~14–15 is the phenotypic-origin estimate across the full 238-tip tree. Reporting a larger number without this distinction overstates the power of the selection inference.

Result — certified hits, v3

The hormone tier: five steroid-synthesis enzymes intensified, one carrier relaxed, zero receptors

HyPhy RELAX on the dichromatic foreground, per-gene BH correction, behind a QC gate (percent alignment gaps, K bounds, foreground n≥3, tree length, alignment-to-reference length ratio in [0.8, 1.2]). Of 17 BH-significant genes, 9 pass QC and are certified; the hormone module supplies 6 of those 9.

Certified — hormone (steroid enzymes)

GeneKpₛₛ
HSD17B12.93<1e-3clean
HSD17B122.52<1e-3clean
SRD5A12.32<1e-3gaps>20% flag
CYP7B11.377.8e-3clean
HSD17B71.324.0e-2clean
SHBG0.242.9e-2relaxed, carrier

Certified — pigmentation (context)

GeneKpₛₛ
TFAP2A1.931e-4clean
KITLG1.301.5e-3clean
EDN31.281.5e-3branch-len flag
Limitation. SRD5A1 (hormone) and EDN3 (pigmentation) clear BH significance and the primary QC gate but carry a secondary alignment flag (SRD5A1: >20% gap content; EDN3: branch-length outlier) — reported as Tier-B, rescue-pending, not withheld. POMC (K=3.1) and TYR (K=2.2) fail QC outright (alignment-to-reference ratio 0.68 / 0.65, truncated extraction) and are not certified hits.

Result — module architecture

Enzymes cluster in the steroid-biosynthesis and androgen/estrogen axes; hormone receptors and the HPG axis are silent

Panel B places every fitted sex-hormone-pathway gene on the same log₂K scale used for pigmentation. The certified hormone hits (bold outline) sit almost entirely on the enzymatic step that interconverts androgens and estrogens — not on the receptors (AR, ESR1, ESR2) or the upstream HPG-axis genes (GNRH1/2, FSHR, LHCGR), which are fitted but not significant, and not on the two QC-rejected artifacts in this region (LHCGR, KISS1R).

Reading panel B

Steroid-biosynthesis row: CYP7B1, HSD17B1, HSD17B7 intensified (bold). Androgen row: SRD5A1 intensified; AR itself (the receptor) is fitted, not significant. Estrogen row: HSD17B12 intensified; ESR1/ESR2 (the receptors) are fitted, not significant. HPG axis and GnRH rows: no significant signal on any gene tested.

Receptors vs. enzymes

Every certified hormone hit acts on hormone supply (synthesis/interconversion) or, for SHBG, hormone transport. None acts on hormone reception. That asymmetry is what the enzyme-concentration claim rests on — it is a pattern in which genes clear BH+QC, not a formal test that enzymes as a class outperform receptors as a class.

The cross-taxon comparison, stated carefully

The bird single-gene switch (MC1R) is absent in primates — it is not "under relaxation," it is not significant

In galliform birds, sexual dichromatism tracks dN/dS at MC1R alone (Nadeau et al. 2007, Proc. R. Soc. B, doi:10.1098/rspb.2007.0174). In this primate dataset, MC1R passes QC cleanly but shows only a weak, uncorrected signal of relaxation: K = 0.45, p = 0.043, and after Benjamini–Hochberg correction pₛₛ = 0.144 — not significant. The correct statement is that the bird model's single-gene switch does not reappear in primates; it is not that MC1R shows confirmed relaxed selection in primates.

MC1R, exact numbers

Testvalue
K (RELAX)0.45
p (uncorrected)0.043
pₛₛ (BH)0.144
QCclean

Why this matters

The absence of an MC1R signal, combined with the presence of a steroid-enzyme signal, is the comparative point: primates do not recapitulate the bird mechanism, and the mechanism they do show is hormone-supply, not melanocortin-receptor, biology.

What this result does not show

Limitations, stated on their own slide

Set-level contrast is not significant

A formal pigmentation-vs-hormone set-level contrast (100,000-permutation label-swap, p ≈ 0.47; Wilcoxon, p ≈ 0.11) does not distinguish the two modules. The finding is about which specific genes, in which specific origins, clear BH+QC — not a claim that hormone genes outweigh pigmentation genes, or vice versa, as gene sets.

Power is origin-limited

Per-origin RELAX is adequately powered in only the 3 clades with ≥2 genomic tips. Single-tip origins are covered by companion aBSREL (per-branch) and RERconverge (relative-rate) analyses and reported, not treated as missing, but they do not carry the same per-origin RELAX power.

POMC is excluded, not negative

POMC (K=3.1) and TYR (K=2.2) show strong apparent intensification but fail alignment QC (aln:ref length ratio 0.68 / 0.65 — truncated extraction) and are excluded from the certified set. POMC is the biologically obvious pigment–hormone bridge (α-MSH and ACTH from one precursor); a denser genome panel is the natural next step to re-test it, not a claim we are making now.

Two flagged Tier-B hits

SRD5A1 (hormone) and EDN3 (pigmentation) clear BH significance and the primary QC gate but carry a secondary alignment flag and are reported as Tier-B, rescue-pending, rather than fully certified.

Researcher-track rubric

Three questions, answered plainly

What did Claude Science help us do

Run and QC-gate a clade-wide, multi-gene HyPhy RELAX screen across 117 primates with per-gene Benjamini–Hochberg correction, and cross-check the same foreground with aBSREL and RERconverge — work that requires coordinating alignment QC, phylogenetic placement, and multiple-testing correction across dozens of genes and four independent clades at once.

How are we closer to a finding

We can now state, with exact test statistics and an explicit QC gate, that the selection signal underlying primate sexual dichromatism is polygenic and splits between a pigmentation module and a sex-hormone module dominated by steroid-metabolism enzymes rather than receptors — a result distinguishable from, and not previously contrasted against, the single-gene bird model.

What is the flagship

The certified hormone-enzyme tier itself: HSD17B1, HSD17B12, HSD17B7, CYP7B1, and SRD5A1 intensified, SHBG relaxed, and AR/ESR1/ESR2 silent — a receptor-null, enzyme-concentrated architecture that is mechanistically coherent for a sex-specific trait and stands independently of the pigmentation side of the same coupled network.

Numbers throughout are from the v3-certified HyPhy RELAX run (117 primates), Benjamini–Hochberg-corrected within gene set, behind the QC gate described above. Bird comparison: Nadeau et al. 2007, Proc. R. Soc. B, doi:10.1098/rspb.2007.0174.
